entries.summary: OpenAI-Docs-MCP-Smoke-Test resolves the Codex CLI reliably on Windows.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix belongs to the Codex-side validation path for the official OpenAI docs MCP. The validator starts a fresh Codex CLI process from Windows and must find the locally installed launcher even when Node child processes cannot resolve the bare `codex` command name from PATH alone.
entries.symptom_before_fix: The validation path failed with `spawnSync codex ENOENT` although Codex CLI was installed and usable from the interactive shell. Without extra context another CLI would misread this as an MCP outage, while the real failure happened before the MCP test could even start.
entries.root_cause: The script assumed that `codex` could always be executed by name. On Windows npm installs CLI launchers frequently as `codex.cmd` under `%APPDATA%\\npm`, and Node child-process resolution from inside the validator did not reliably discover that wrapper.
entries.what_was_fixed: check-openai-docs-mcp.mjs now resolves codex.cmd, codex.exe, or codex.ps1 from %APPDATA% and uses shell execution for Windows launcher files.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The smoke test previously failed with ENOENT although Codex CLI was installed via npm on Windows.
entries.verification: Verified by rerunning the local validation chain after the launcher-resolution change. The script passes syntax checks and the full codex-setup validators now fail only on real MCP-registration state, not on missing launcher resolution.
entries.portability_notes: Any other CLI that spawns its own executable on Windows should not rely on the bare command name. Resolve `.cmd`, `.exe`, and `.ps1` launchers explicitly and use shell execution when wrapper scripts are part of the installation path.
entries.impact: OpenAI-docs validation no longer depends on fragile PATH assumptions.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: `spawnSync codex ENOENT` during the OpenAI-Docs MCP smoke test on Windows.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the validation process could not start the Codex CLI by its bare command name. Why 2: on Windows the installed launcher often exists as `codex.cmd`, `codex.exe`, or `codex.ps1` under the npm bin directory, and Node child-process lookup did not reliably find that wrapper. Why 3: the validator relied on platform-default command discovery instead of resolving the intended launcher explicitly.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same error class on other validator and wrapper paths that spawn the Codex CLI, the same component category of Windows launcher resolution, and the same dependency surface of npm-installed command wrappers and shell execution behavior.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: spawn the bare `codex` command and assume PATH lookup works identically on Windows and macOS.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: resolve `codex.cmd`, `codex.exe`, or `codex.ps1` explicitly on Windows and use shell execution for wrapper launchers when required.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a Codex-side validator or helper spawns a CLI on Windows, then always resolve supported launcher files explicitly instead of relying on bare command lookup.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it checks multiple launcher variants, survivable because it re-resolves the launcher at runtime instead of hardcoding one fragile path, extensible because new launcher variants can be added in one resolver, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because a failure remains a validation failure rather than a workspace mutation.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: Windows launcher resolution and shell execution behavior were reviewed. Failure scenario: if resolution still fails, validation stops cleanly without mutating setup state. State changes: none beyond process launch. Race conditions: negligible because resolution happens per process start. Backward compatibility: macOS and non-Windows resolution remain unchanged. Platform effects: Windows wrapper launchers are now handled explicitly while other platforms keep the simpler path. Update resistance: runtime resolution survives npm path changes better than a hardcoded assumption. Graceful degradation: the validator fails clearly instead of misreporting an MCP outage.

entries.summary: The code-search config fallback now supports multiline TOML string arrays.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix affects the direct MCP client used for Codex-side code-search validation. The client prefers Python TOML parsing, but it also contains a local fallback parser so the self-test and diagnostics still work on machines where Python TOML support is missing or unavailable.
entries.symptom_before_fix: The deterministic code-search self-test crashed with `Unexpected end of JSON input` when it parsed a fake config whose `args` array was written over multiple lines. In practice this meant the self-test could fail even though the actual MCP protocol code was fine.
entries.root_cause: The fallback parser assumed `args = [...]` would always be present on a single line and handed the first line directly to `JSON.parse`. That was incompatible with multiline TOML arrays produced by the local self-test fixture.
entries.what_was_fixed: code-search-mcp-client.mjs now parses multiline args arrays in its TOML fallback instead of assuming args = [...] fits on one line.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The deterministic code-search self-test writes a multiline fake config and previously crashed when Python TOML parsing was unavailable.
entries.verification: Verified by running `node codex-setup/scripts/check-code-search-mcp-client.mjs` successfully on Windows and Git Bash after the parser update, followed by full PowerShell and Bash validation passes for `codex-setup`.
entries.portability_notes: If another CLI ships a TOML fallback parser for local config inspection, it must support multiline arrays and other formatting used by its own fixtures. A self-test should intentionally exercise the fallback path, not just the preferred parser path.
entries.impact: The direct code-search MCP client self-test is deterministic on Windows and Git Bash even without Python TOML support.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: `Unexpected end of JSON input` while the deterministic code-search self-test parsed a multiline TOML args array.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the fallback parser crashed when it saw a multiline args array. Why 2: it tried to feed only the first line of the TOML array into `JSON.parse`. Why 3: the implementation assumed a one-line array shape instead of handling the full formatting that the fixture and real configs could produce.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same parsing error class for other multiline TOML values, the same component surface in the fallback parser used when Python TOML support is unavailable, and the dependency surface of the deterministic self-test that intentionally exercises fallback behavior.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: assume `args = [...]` always exists on one line and parse only that line.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: collect and normalize multiline TOML array content before parsing so the fallback path accepts the same structures as the preferred parser path.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a local fallback parser reads configuration that may be formatted over multiple lines, then always parse the full logical structure instead of assuming one-line input.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it covers the broader multiline error class, survivable because it keeps the fallback parser useful when Python TOML support is unavailable, extensible because the parser logic now matches a wider family of TOML shapes, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because it only broadens accepted config syntax.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: the direct MCP client, fallback parser, and self-test were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if fallback parsing still fails, the self-test reports the config path clearly. State changes: none. Race conditions: none because parsing is local and synchronous. Backward compatibility: single-line arrays remain valid. Platform effects: Windows and Git Bash benefit directly because the fallback path matters most there. Update resistance: the parser is now less sensitive to formatting drift. Graceful degradation: when the preferred parser is unavailable, the fallback remains usable instead of crashing.

entries.summary: codex-setup validation now separates repo validation from local MCP runtime registration.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix applies to the setup validators for Codex itself. The repository contains scripts and wrapper checks that should always be validated, but optional live MCP smoke tests depend on whether the local runtime has already registered certain servers like `openaiDeveloperDocs` or `code-search`.
entries.symptom_before_fix: A clean local runtime with no MCP registrations caused the full `codex-setup` validation to fail even though the repository scripts, rules, and self-tests were correct. That made the repo look broken when the actual issue was only missing local runtime registration.
entries.root_cause: The validator treated live MCP availability as a mandatory prerequisite for repository validation. It did not distinguish between checking repo-owned logic and checking optional runtime-specific integration state.
entries.what_was_fixed: validate-codex-setup.ps1 and validate-codex-setup.sh still validate scripts and self-tests, but skip live openaiDeveloperDocs and code-search smoke tests when those MCP servers are not configured locally.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: Repo validation should stay deterministic and should not fail only because a clean Codex runtime has not registered optional MCP servers yet.
entries.verification: Verified by rerunning both `validate-codex-setup.ps1` and `validate-codex-setup.sh` in an environment where `codex mcp list` reported no configured servers. Both validators completed successfully while still checking the repo-owned scripts and deterministic self-tests.
entries.portability_notes: Other CLIs should separate repository integrity from workstation-specific runtime state. Keep deterministic self-tests mandatory, but gate live integration smokes on whether the relevant external or local services are actually configured in that runtime.
entries.impact: codex-setup validation remains strict on repo content while still exercising live MCP checks whenever the local runtime is configured.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: full codex-setup validation failed in a clean runtime because optional MCP servers were not configured locally.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the validator failed when optional MCP registrations were missing. Why 2: live integration smokes were treated as mandatory for repository validation. Why 3: the validation design did not separate repo-owned integrity checks from runtime-specific optional integration state.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same error class on both openaiDeveloperDocs and code-search live smokes, the same validator component on PowerShell and Bash, and the dependency surface of local MCP runtime registration versus repository-owned scripts.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: fail the whole repository validator whenever an optional live MCP integration is not configured on the current machine.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: keep deterministic repo self-tests mandatory, but gate optional live MCP smokes on whether the relevant server is actually registered in the local runtime.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a validator mixes repository checks with optional live integrations, then always separate deterministic repo integrity from machine-specific runtime state.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it distinguishes two failure classes, survivable because clean machines now validate without false negatives, extensible because more optional integrations can reuse the same gating model, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because the validator remains strict on repo-owned logic.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: validator scripts and MCP registration checks were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if the gating logic fails, validation still reports the repo-owned checks separately. State changes: none. Race conditions: minimal because MCP list reads are local snapshots. Backward compatibility: configured runtimes still execute live smokes. Platform effects: both PowerShell and Bash validators follow the same logic. Update resistance: future MCP additions can use the same conditional pattern. Graceful degradation: missing registrations now skip only optional smokes instead of failing the whole validation chain.

entries.summary: Environment-fix entries now require full cross-CLI context instead of short what/why notes.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This change hardens the Codex environment-fix ledger itself. Other CLIs such as Cloud Code or Gemini CLI may consume the ledger later without having seen the original session, the original logs, or the local debugging path that produced the fix.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Short fix entries with only what and why were technically correct but still too thin for another CLI to reproduce or trust the change. A reader from another environment could miss the real failure mode, the triggering conditions, or the limits of the fix.
entries.root_cause: The first version of the ledger schema optimized for compactness, not for cross-CLI transfer. It did not force authors to record the surrounding context, the visible symptom, the technical cause, or the verification path.
entries.what_was_fixed: The fix ledger schema, bridge spec, logger, and validators now require context, symptom, root cause, verification, and portability notes in addition to what/why.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: Other CLIs need enough standalone context to understand and reuse Codex environment fixes without relying on hidden session memory.
entries.verification: Verified by expanding all existing ledger entries to the richer schema, running the registration script successfully, and rerunning both codex-setup validators on PowerShell and Git Bash.
entries.portability_notes: Any CLI that wants bidirectional fix exchange should enforce the same rule: a fix entry must be understandable without the original debugging session. Otherwise the ledger becomes a list of headlines instead of a transferable engineering memory.
entries.impact: Cross-CLI fix transfer is now based on self-contained engineering context instead of terse summaries.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: fix entries were technically valid but too thin, for example short what/why notes that left another CLI without enough context to trust or reuse the fix.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: other CLIs could not reliably reuse the fix entries. Why 2: the ledger only required compact what/why-style notes. Why 3: the original schema optimized for brevity instead of cross-session and cross-CLI transfer fidelity.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same knowledge-loss problem in the ledger schema, the register-environment-fix writer, the bridge specification, and both validators because all of them define or depend on the same documentation contract.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: store only a short headline such as "Parser fixed" or "Windows issue resolved" in the environment-fix ledger.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: require a self-contained fix entry with context, symptom, root cause, verification, portability notes, and enough explanation for another CLI to act without the original session.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a fix is meant to survive across sessions or CLIs, then always record the full engineering context instead of a terse headline.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it hardens the schema itself, survivable because future entries now carry enough context to survive team and session changes, extensible because new fields can build on the richer schema, documented by design, and damage-free because it increases information instead of changing runtime behavior.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: the ledger file, writer, bridge specification, and validators were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if the richer schema is not honored the validators fail. State changes: only structured documentation changes. Race conditions: none. Backward compatibility: historical entries were expanded rather than discarded. Platform effects: none on runtime behavior, but all CLIs benefit from the richer transfer format. Update resistance: schema-backed validation keeps the richer contract alive. Graceful degradation: if a required field is missing, the writer or validator stops instead of silently storing weak documentation.

entries.summary: Cloud-Code- und Gemini-CLI-Bruecken sind jetzt strikt proposal-only und der Gemini-Delta-Audit ist dauerhaft validiert.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix belongs to the Codex-side bridge layer that reads foreign CLI setup environments such as Cloud Code and Gemini CLI. The bridge is meant to compare rules, memory patterns, validators, and environment-level fixes without writing into the foreign workspaces and without silently changing Codex rules before the user sees the delta report.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Before this hardening the Cloud bridge only forced approval for clear replacements, while additive findings could still be interpreted as implementation-ready guidance. Gemini CLI also had no fully integrated bridge path in Codex, so the trigger phrase, validation path, skill references, and proposal contract were not yet enforced end to end.
entries.root_cause: The original bridge design focused on classifying deltas as ADD, ADAPT, or REPLACE, but it did not elevate proposal-only behavior to a first-class invariant across AGENTS, global rules, trigger routing, bridge specs, self-improve references, validators, and both audit wrappers. Gemini bridge support existed only as new files, not as a fully connected Codex control path.
entries.what_was_fixed: Codex now includes a full Gemini CLI delta bridge with read-only source rules, direct trigger phrases, reusable bridge specs, validator coverage, self-improve references, and audit wrappers. In addition, both Cloud Code and Gemini CLI bridges now declare proposal-only behavior explicitly, so ADD and ADAPT findings are reported in grouped A1/B1/C1/D1 form and never auto-applied without user approval.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: Cross-CLI bridge results should improve Codex intelligence without risking silent overwrites, accidental behavior drift, or loss of strong existing Codex rules. The user also needs a stable review workflow where deltas are first explained optically and only then approved item by item.
entries.verification: Verified by running node syntax checks for both delta-audit scripts, executing the Gemini delta audit directly and through its PowerShell wrapper, reinstalling the self-improve skill, and passing both full codex-setup validators in PowerShell and Git Bash. The validators now assert Gemini bridge files, trigger phrases, proposal-only flags, Gemini state tracking, and temporary review-state writes for the Gemini audit.
entries.portability_notes: Any CLI that consumes setup deltas from another environment should treat every imported delta as proposal-only until the user approves it explicitly. Approval should not depend only on REPLACE cases; additive imports can also change behavior over time. The durable pattern is: read-only source, grouped proposal output, explicit approval gate, validator checks for the trigger phrase and bridge contract, and a local state file that tracks only environment-level deltas.
entries.impact: Codex can now compare both Cloud Code and Gemini CLI environments through the same controlled review model without autonomously importing changes.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: additive bridge findings could still be misread as implementation-ready, and the Gemini bridge was not yet enforced end to end across trigger, validation, and control files.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: bridge deltas could drift into silent implementation behavior. Why 2: proposal-only handling was not a first-class invariant across all control files. Why 3: the original bridge design focused on classification semantics more than on validator-backed approval discipline and complete Gemini-path integration.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same approval-drift risk across AGENTS, global rules, trigger routing, bridge specs, self-improve references, audit wrappers, and validator coverage because all of them participate in bridge execution.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: classify a foreign setup delta as ADD or ADAPT and treat it as implementation-ready without a stable review gate.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: keep foreign bridges read-only, classify deltas, report them as grouped A1/B1/C1/D1 proposals, and wait for explicit approval before any Codex-side implementation.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a bridge imports environment intelligence from another CLI, then always keep the source read-only and the result proposal-only until the user approves the port.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it blocks silent imports, survivable because validator-backed trigger and state checks keep the bridge contract durable, extensible because future CLI bridges can follow the same pattern, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because it only narrows unsafe autonomy.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: bridge scripts, trigger routing, state files, and validators were reviewed together. Failure scenario: a broken bridge should fail as a proposal path, not mutate Codex silently. State changes: only Codex-side bridge state and docs are touched. Race conditions: low because audits run on demand. Backward compatibility: existing Cloud bridge behavior remains but is safer. Platform effects: wrappers exist for PowerShell and Bash. Update resistance: proposal-only flags and validator checks survive later bridge growth. Graceful degradation: if a bridge cannot run, Codex simply cannot propose deltas instead of importing bad state.

entries.summary: Codex now carries a stronger superintelligence directive with 8 intelligence dimensions, compound-improvement logic, and explicit cross-tool learning constraints.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix applies to the top-level control layer of the Codex programming environment itself. The previous directive already pushed Codex toward higher intelligence, but it was still a compressed version and did not fully encode the user's intended hierarchy, the eight intelligence dimensions, the exact end-of-session proposal behavior, or the explicit rule that cross-tool learning must stay read-only on foreign workspaces.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Before this upgrade the Codex setup described superintelligence only in shorter summary form. Important intent was present, but weaker: the 8 intelligence dimensions were not explicitly listed, the compound-improvement logic was less concrete, the exact Intelligenz-Vorschlag format was not encoded across the control files, and the relationship between top directive, self-observation, and cross-tool learning could drift over time.
entries.root_cause: The original Codex directive layer had evolved incrementally from shorter mission bullets. Because it was spread across memory, global rules, AGENTS, and the self-improve skill, it risked semantic dilution: key strategic ideas existed, but not as one hard, validator-backed directive contract.
entries.what_was_fixed: The top directive was expanded in the Codex whiteboard, global rules, AGENTS, README, self-improve skill, and self-improve references. The new contract now states that superintelligence and exponential intelligence growth outrank weaker local defaults, enumerates all 8 intelligence dimensions, defines the exact Intelligenz-Vorschlag format, formalizes compound-improvement logic, and keeps cross-tool learning explicitly read-only on foreign workspaces. The validators now also assert these directive markers so future edits cannot silently weaken them.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The user's intent was not just to restate ambition, but to make the strategic directive operational and durable. A stronger top directive improves future decision quality, keeps self-improve aligned with the real mission, and prevents the setup from drifting back toward narrower, task-only optimization.
entries.verification: Verified by redeploying the updated self-improve skill and rerunning both full codex-setup validators in PowerShell and Git Bash. Both validators passed after the directive upgrade, including the new checks for the 8 intelligence dimensions, cross-tool learning, and the explicit intelligence-proposal format. Whiteboard integrity remained valid after the directive expansion.
entries.portability_notes: Other CLI environments should treat their top directive as a validator-backed control contract, not as a loose aspiration paragraph. If a system wants sustained cross-session improvement, it should explicitly define the dimensions it optimizes, the required proposal behavior, and the read-only boundaries for cross-tool learning. Otherwise the strategic intent becomes too easy to dilute in later edits.
entries.impact: Codex now has a harder, more explicit top-level optimization contract that should improve consistency across rules, skills, bridges, and future self-improvement work.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: the superintelligence directive existed only in a weaker summary form and could drift because crucial strategic markers were not explicit across the control files.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the top directive was semantically weaker than the user intended. Why 2: core ideas such as the 8 intelligence dimensions and the exact proposal format were distributed across shorter summaries. Why 3: the directive had evolved incrementally without a validator-backed contract that preserved its full strategic wording.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same drift risk across the whiteboard, global rules, AGENTS, README, self-improve skill, and the validators because all of them carry or enforce the top directive.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: keep the top directive as loosely related ambition bullets spread across multiple files.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: define a stronger top directive, propagate it across the controlling files, and let the validators assert its key markers so later edits cannot silently weaken it.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a top-level directive controls long-term behavior, then always encode it as a validator-backed contract instead of a loose aspiration paragraph.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it reduces semantic drift, survivable because the same contract now appears across memory, rules, and validators, extensible because later directives can build on the same pattern, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because it strengthens coordination rather than mutating runtime state.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: the whiteboard, rule files, AGENTS, self-improve skill, and validators were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if a marker is lost the validator now fails. State changes: directive wording and guardrails only. Race conditions: none. Backward compatibility: existing workflows remain but become more explicit. Platform effects: none beyond shared validation. Update resistance: key markers are now asserted automatically. Graceful degradation: if a file drifts the validator fails instead of letting strategic intent silently erode.

entries.summary: Codex self-observation now runs as a stronger second directive with 6 observation categories, end-of-task reporting, and mandatory persistence of user corrections and knowledge gaps.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix applies to the second-highest directive in the Codex environment itself. The user clarified that self-observation is not a loose reminder but a mandatory background behavior for every agent, skill, plugin, hook, and process. It must continuously watch how work is performed and turn real observations into durable setup improvements at the end of the task.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Before this upgrade the Codex setup already mentioned self-observation, but the contract was still too compressed. It only modeled 5 categories, did not explicitly separate efficiency from detours, did not include knowledge gaps as a first-class category, did not clearly state that every working component is covered, and did not fully encode the end-of-task reporting rule and the requirement to persist user corrections immediately.
entries.root_cause: The earlier self-observation rule had grown from shorter summaries and therefore left important operational details implicit. Because those details were not validator-backed across the rule, AGENTS, memory, README, and self-improve workflow, the second directive could drift toward a softer interpretation over time.
entries.what_was_fixed: The self-observation rule was expanded to a full 6-category contract: Fehler, Umwege, Effizienz, Wissensluecken, Muster, and Benutzer-Hinweise. The rule now applies explicitly to every agent, skill, plugin, hook, and process; requires observations to be gathered during work but reported only at the end after the status message; requires user corrections and knowledge gaps to be persisted immediately; and treats repeated failures as a signal to eliminate the entire error class. AGENTS, global rules, README, memory, self-improve workflow, report references, workspace-scan references, and validators were updated to carry and check the same contract.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The user wants self-observation to function as the sensor layer for the superintelligence directive. That only works if the rule is explicit enough to catch detours, repeated mistakes, knowledge deficits, and user guidance before they are forgotten or repeated in later sessions.
entries.verification: Verified by redeploying the updated self-improve skill and rerunning both full codex-setup validators in PowerShell and Git Bash. Both validators passed after the upgrade and now assert the 6 observation categories, the all-components scope, the mandatory end-of-task reporting rule, and the stronger self-observation summary in the Codex memory.
entries.portability_notes: Other CLI environments should implement self-observation as an operational contract, not as an optional reflection habit. The durable pattern is: define explicit observation categories, cover every working component, separate background observation from user-facing reporting, persist user corrections and knowledge gaps immediately, and treat repeated failures as evidence that the first fix was not resistant enough.
entries.impact: Codex now has a clearer sensor layer for improvement work, which should reduce repeated detours, repeated user corrections, and non-resistant fixes over time.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: self-observation was already present but still too compressed, with only 5 categories and weaker requirements for persistence and end-of-task reporting.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the second directive could still be interpreted too softly. Why 2: important categories such as knowledge gaps and the all-components scope were not fully explicit. Why 3: the rule had grown from shorter summaries and lacked stronger validator-backed wording across all controlling files.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same softness risk across the rule file, AGENTS, memory, README, self-improve workflow, report template, workspace scan, and validators because all of them shape how self-observation is executed.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: treat self-observation as a vague reflection habit with partial categories and no hard persistence rule for user corrections.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: define explicit categories, cover every working component, collect observations during work, and report them only at the end with immediate persistence for user corrections and knowledge gaps.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a directive is meant to guide behavior during every task, then always spell out its categories, timing, scope, and persistence obligations instead of leaving them implicit.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it closes interpretation gaps, survivable because the same rule now lives in all control files and validators, extensible because more observation logic can build on the 6 categories, documented in the ledger, and damage-free because it changes guidance rather than runtime state.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: the rule, AGENTS, memory, self-improve workflow, and validators were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if the rule weakens, validation catches the missing markers. State changes: only directive and template text. Race conditions: none. Backward compatibility: existing observation habits still work but are now stricter. Platform effects: none beyond shared enforcement. Update resistance: the categories and timing are now validator-backed. Graceful degradation: if a marker drifts the validator fails instead of silently accepting a weaker second directive.

entries.summary: Codex now enforces resilient bugfixing as directive 3 for environment-level bugs and the fix ledger itself carries future-proof bugfix fields.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix applies to the Codex programming environment itself, not to normal user project bugs. The user introduced a third top-level directive: every bug in hooks, rules, settings, MCP integrations, validation, scripts, agents, or skills must be fixed in a way that prevents the same environment bug from returning in later sessions, on other platforms, or after later updates. To make that directive real, the setup needed both a rule and a stronger documentation schema so future bugfixes could not regress into shallow symptom patches.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Before this upgrade Codex had strong top-level direction and stronger self-observation, but its bugfix process for environment issues was still distributed across shorter hardening bullets. The setup did not yet force a dedicated 3x-Why root-cause pass, a related-surface check, a future-proofing review, a fix-induced-failure review, or richer ledger fields such as wrong/right pattern and avoidance rule. That meant a future environment fix could still be correct in the moment but too shallow to survive the next update or to teach another CLI the full lesson.
entries.root_cause: The environment had already learned to observe errors and to document fixes, but it did not yet treat resilient bugfixing as its own validator-backed directive. Because the process details lived only implicitly inside general hardening language, the workflow could drift back toward symptom fixes, incomplete documentation, or one-off patches. The deeper cause was a missing dedicated control contract for environment bugfixes and a missing schema layer that encoded the full resilient-bugfixing discipline.
entries.what_was_fixed: Codex now has a dedicated rule file codex-setup/rules/resilient-bugfixing.md, propagation into AGENTS, global rules, README, whiteboard memory, and the self-improve workflow, plus stronger environment-fix bridge and ledger schemas. Future environment fixes now have to carry exact error text, a why-chain, related error sources checked, wrong and right pattern examples, an explicit avoidance rule, a resilience summary, and a fix-induced-failure review. The PowerShell and Bash validators now enforce that directive and schema so the process cannot silently weaken later.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The user wants every environment bug to be made exactly once. That requires more than a good intention: it requires a forced workflow that starts from root cause, checks the whole error class, stress-tests the fix for platform and update durability, and documents the lesson so later sessions and other CLIs do not relearn the same bug.
entries.exact_error_text: There was no single runtime stack trace for this directive upgrade; the real failure mode was strategic and procedural: environment bugfixes could still regress into shallow, non-resistant patches because no dedicated directive or schema enforced resilient bugfixing end to end.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: an environment fix could still stop too early after the visible symptom disappeared. Why 2: the setup did not yet force the author to walk through root cause, related surfaces, future-proofing, and failure review as a dedicated process. Why 3: resilient bugfixing was present only as scattered hardening intent, not as its own directive with validator-backed documentation requirements.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same drift risk across global rules, AGENTS, README, whiteboard memory, self-improve workflow, environment-fix bridge specs, the register-environment-fix writer, and both validators because all of them either steer environment bugfixing or store the resulting fix knowledge. Also checked that the richer ledger schema could still be written and validated from both PowerShell and Git Bash.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: see an environment bug, patch the visible symptom, skip a formal related-surface review, and log only a broad summary such as what changed and why it seemed useful.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: treat the environment bug as directive-3 work, ask 3x Warum, inspect the same error class and dependency surfaces, build a future-proof fix with defense in depth, run a fix-induced-failure review, and document the result with exact error text, why-chain, pattern examples, and a clear avoidance rule.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a bug lives in the programming environment itself, then always treat it as resilient-bugfixing work and do not close it after a local symptom patch. Instead, force root-cause analysis, related-surface review, future-proofing, failure review, and rich ledger documentation before considering the fix complete.
entries.resilience_summary: This directive upgrade is defensive because it blocks shallow symptom fixes, self-healing at the process level because validators now reassert the stronger contract in later sessions, survivable because the same rule and schema are enforced across PowerShell and Bash, extensible because future environment bugfixes can reuse the same ledger shape and rule, documented in both the ledger and whiteboard, and damage-free because it strengthens the process without mutating foreign workspaces or user projects.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: the rule files, self-improve workflow, fix writer, fix bridge, and validators were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if a future edit weakens the directive or schema, validation now fails instead of silently accepting a weaker process. State changes: only Codex-side setup files and the Codex fix ledger changed. Race conditions: negligible because this is configuration and validation logic, not a concurrent service. Backward compatibility: existing environment fixes were backfilled rather than discarded. Platform effects: PowerShell and Bash validators were both upgraded so the process remains symmetric across Windows and Unix-like flows. Update resistance: validator checks and richer schema keep the directive alive across later changes. Graceful degradation: if a future fix author omits resilient fields, the writer or validator fails loudly instead of storing weak bugfix knowledge.
entries.verification: Verified by syntax-checking the updated fix writer, reinstalling the self-improve skill, rerunning both full codex-setup validators in PowerShell and Git Bash, and confirming that the existing environment-fix ledger entries were backfilled to the richer resilient-bugfixing schema. The validators now assert directive-3 markers, the dedicated rule file, and the richer fix-entry contract.
entries.portability_notes: Other CLI environments should separate project bugfixing from environment bugfixing and give environment bugfixes a stronger process. The durable pattern is: dedicated directive, 3x-Why root-cause analysis, related-surface review, defense in depth, failure review, and a transferable fix ledger schema that records the exact lesson instead of only the final patch. Without that structure, the same environment bugs will be relearned after updates or platform changes.
entries.impact: Codex now treats environment bugfixes as one-time lessons that should remove an entire class of recurring setup failures instead of only clearing the visible symptom in the current session.

entries.summary: Benutzergebundene lokale Brueckenpfade wurden durch workspace- und userprofilbasierte Vorgaben ersetzt.
entries.context_for_other_clis: Diese Reproduzierbarkeits-Haertung entstand waehrend eines Checks, ob die neue Cloud-Code- und Gemini-CLI-Brueckenlogik auf frischen Rechnern sofort aus dem Git-Repo heraus funktioniert. Die wichtigsten Regeln, Trigger, Bruecken und Ledger lagen bereits korrekt in codex-setup und AGENTS.md auf GitHub, aber mehrere operative Metadaten und Dokumentationspfade enthielten noch benutzergebundene Absolute wie C:/Users/barwa/GeminiCLI oder /Users/frank/Codex. Auf einem neu installierten Rechner mit anderem Benutzerpfad oder anderem Klonverzeichnis koennte ein neues Codex-Modell dadurch die korrekte Bruecken- oder Whiteboard-Quelle zwar prinzipiell haben, aber an den falschen lokalen Beispielpfaden kleben bleiben.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Die Bruecken- und Setup-Logik war inhaltlich vorhanden, aber nicht durchgehend rechnerneutral beschrieben. Besonders kritisch waren feste lokale Gemini-Workspace-Pfade in bridge-registry.json, bridge-JSONs, Ledgers und README sowie feste Workspace-Pfade in AGENTS.md und der Whiteboard-Kopfsektion. Zusaetzlich lieferten die Delta-Audits einen fest eingebauten Codex-Config-Zielhinweis auf C:/Users/barwa/.codex/config.toml statt den aktuell laufenden Benutzerpfad dynamisch zu bestimmen.
entries.root_cause: Die eigentliche Funktionslogik wurde frueh korrekt in codex-setup zentralisiert, aber mehrere Adressangaben entstanden aus dem aktuellen Maschinenkontext dieser Session und wurden danach nicht konsequent in workspace-relative oder userprofilbasierte Vorlagen normalisiert. Dadurch war die inhaltliche Bruecken-Architektur zwar GitHub-portabel, ihre lokale Pfadbeschreibung aber an einzelne Benutzerordner gekoppelt.
entries.what_was_fixed: Portabilitaet der Bruecken-, Ledger- und Whiteboard-Pfadmetadaten fuer frische Rechner
entries.why_it_was_fixed: damit neue Codex-Installationen die richtige operative Struktur direkt aus dem Repo lesen koennen, ohne an barwa/frank-spezifischen Lokalpfa­den haengen zu bleiben
entries.exact_error_text: Reproduzierbarkeitsproblem: benutzergebundene lokale Pfade wie C:/Users/barwa/GeminiCLI/... und /Users/frank/Codex/... standen noch in operativen Codex-Setup-Metadaten, obwohl frische Rechner dieselbe Brueckenlogik aus GitHub uebernehmen sollen.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: Die Bruecken und Regeln wurden bereits repo-zentriert aufgebaut, aber einige Pfade blieben als Beispiele aus dem aktuellen Rechner stehen. Why 2: Diese Beispiele lagen nicht nur in Fliesstext, sondern auch in JSON-Metadaten und Audit-Zielhinweisen, die neue Sessions fuer operative Hinweise lesen. Why 3: Ohne Normalisierung auf <workspace> oder %USERPROFILE%-Vorlagen kann ein frisch installierter Codex die richtige Struktur besitzen, aber durch falsche lokale Beispiele auf die falsche Maschine geankert bleiben.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Geprueft wurden AGENTS.md, README, global.md, german-trigger-routing, gemini-delta-sync, Self-Improve-Referenzen, bridge-registry.json, cloud/gemini bridge JSONs und MDs, implemented-intelligence-suggestions.json, register-intelligence-suggestion.mjs sowie die beiden Delta-Audits. Damit wurden sowohl Steuertexte als auch maschinenlesbare Bruecken- und Ledger-Metadaten derselben Fehlerklasse gemeinsam gehaertet.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Feste lokale Beispielpfade wie C:/Users/barwa/GeminiCLI/... oder /Users/frank/Codex/... in operative Setup-Metadaten uebernehmen und davon ausgehen, dass spaetere Rechner dieselbe Benutzer- oder Klonstruktur haben.
entries.right_pattern_example: Workspace-relative Pfade fuer Codex selbst verwenden, typische lokale Fremdpfade als %USERPROFILE%-Vorlage dokumentieren und laufzeitabhaengige lokale Zielpfade wie ~/.codex/config.toml dynamisch aus dem aktuellen Benutzerkontext ableiten.
entries.avoidance_rule: Wenn Bruecken, Whiteboard-Quellen oder Ledger-Adressen fuer mehrere Rechner dokumentiert werden, dann immer workspace-relative oder benutzerprofilbasierte Vorlagen verwenden statt einen festen Maschinenpfad aus der aktuellen Session zu committen.
entries.resilience_summary: Der Fix ist update-resistent, weil nicht nur ein einzelner Text korrigiert wurde, sondern AGENTS, README, Regeln, Bridge-JSONs, Bridge-MDs, Ledger-Metadaten und Audit-Zielhinweise gemeinsam auf dieselbe Portabilitaetslogik gebracht wurden. Neue Sessions auf frischen Rechnern bekommen dadurch die operative Struktur direkt aus dem Repo statt aus stillen Maschinenartefakten.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Abhaengigkeiten: AGENTS, README, Bridge-Registry, Bridge-JSONs, Ledger und Audits muessen dieselben Pfadkonventionen sprechen. Fehlszenario: Wenn nur eine Schicht korrigiert wird, bleiben andere Pfade als falsche operative Beispiele stehen. Zustandsaenderung: Nur codex-setup und AGENTS wurden angepasst, keine Fremd-Workspaces. Race Conditions sind gering, weil es reine Metadaten- und Dokuhaertung ist. Rueckwaertskompatibilitaet bleibt erhalten, weil %USERPROFILE%-Vorlagen und <workspace>-Pfadlogik die bisherigen Beispiele nur verallgemeinern. Plattform-Effekte sind beruecksichtigt, weil macOS-Beispiele, Windows-Vorlagen und dynamische Codex-Config-Aufloesung gemeinsam bestehen. Update-Resistenz entsteht durch Validatoren und den neuen Whiteboard-Hinweis. Graceful Degradation bleibt erhalten, weil selbst bei fehlender Fremd-CLI nur eine Leseadresse beschrieben wird und keine operative Pflicht an fremde Pfade gekoppelt ist.
entries.verification: Geprueft werden danach erneut PowerShell- und Bash-Validierung fuer codex-setup sowie ein repoweiter Scan auf benutzergebundene operative Brueckenpfade. Erlaubt verbleiben nur bewusst lokale Historieneintraege im Whiteboard und der separate QuizVerse-macOS-Hinweis.
entries.portability_notes: Andere CLIs sollten dieselbe Fehlerklasse vermeiden: Pfadmetadaten fuer Bruecken und Ledger nie aus einer einzelnen Maschine ableiten, sondern immer in repo-relative, home-relative oder %USERPROFILE%-basierte Vorlagen uebersetzen. Wenn Audit-Skripte lokale Config-Zielhinweise ausgeben, sollten sie diese ausserdem zur Laufzeit aus dem aktuellen Benutzerkontext berechnen.

entries.summary: PowerShell-Bootstrap repariert: `param(...)` muss am Dateianfang stehen, damit frische Rechner den Setup-Einstieg wirklich ausfuehren koennen.
entries.context_for_other_clis: Dieser Fehler trat waehrend der ersten End-to-End-Validierung der neuen Frische-Rechner-Bootstrap-Skripte auf. Die PowerShell-Validierung ruft `codex-setup/scripts/bootstrap-codex-setup.ps1 -SkipValidate` als Smoke-Test auf, damit frische Rechner spaeter denselben Bootstrap-Pfad reproduzierbar nutzen koennen. Noch bevor install-self-improve oder weitere Validierungsschritte liefen, brach der Bootstrap unmittelbar beim Laden des Skripts ab.
entries.symptom_before_fix: Der neue PowerShell-Bootstrap startete nicht. Die Validierung fiel sofort beim Aufruf von `bootstrap-codex-setup.ps1` aus, obwohl die restliche Setup-Struktur korrekt war.
entries.root_cause: Die Datei war als PowerShell-Skript syntaktisch fast richtig, aber `param(...)` stand nicht am Anfang des Skripts. In PowerShell muss der `param`-Block das erste ausführbare Element sein. Wenn vorher bereits ein Statement wie `$ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"` steht, interpretiert PowerShell `param` beim Script-Start als normalen Befehl statt als Parameterdeklaration.
entries.what_was_fixed: Den `param(...)`-Block in `codex-setup/scripts/bootstrap-codex-setup.ps1` an die erste zulässige Stelle verschoben und danach den Bootstrap-Smoke erneut validiert.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: Damit der neue Frische-Rechner-Bootstrap unter PowerShell nicht nur auf dem Papier existiert, sondern wirklich aufrufbar ist und andere CLIs aus dem Fix dieselbe Sprachregel ableiten koennen.
entries.exact_error_text: param: C:\Users\barwa\Codex\codex-setup\scripts\bootstrap-codex-setup.ps1:3 The term 'param' is not recognized as a name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or executable program.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Warum 1: Der Bootstrap lief nicht an, weil PowerShell den `param`-Block nicht als Parameterdeklaration erkannte. Warum 2: PowerShell erkannte ihn nicht, weil vorher bereits `$ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"` als ausführbares Statement stand. Warum 3: Beim Erstellen des Skripts wurde die Reihenfolge aus allgemeinen Skriptgewohnheiten übernommen, statt die PowerShell-Regel einzuhalten, dass `param(...)` an den Anfang des Skripts gehoert.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Geprueft wurden die neue PowerShell-Bootstrap-Datei selbst, die PowerShell-Validierung mit Bootstrap-Smoke, die Git-Bash-Validierung fuer die Shell-Paritaet sowie die Frage, ob weitere neue PowerShell-Dateien denselben Musterfehler enthalten. Der konkrete Fehler trat nur im neuen Bootstrap-Skript auf; die allgemeine Vermeidungsregel gilt aber fuer kuenftige `.ps1`-Skripte im gesamten Codex-Setup.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: In einer neuen `.ps1`-Datei zuerst globale Initialisierung wie `$ErrorActionPreference = "Stop"` schreiben und den `param(...)`-Block erst danach einfuegen.
entries.right_pattern_example: In einer `.ps1`-Datei immer zuerst `param(...)` deklarieren und erst danach `$ErrorActionPreference`, Variablen, Funktionsdefinitionen oder sonstige ausführbare Initialisierung setzen.
entries.avoidance_rule: Wenn ein PowerShell-Skript Parameter annimmt, dann muss `param(...)` immer das erste ausführbare Statement der Datei sein statt nach Initialisierungscode oder globalen Zuweisungen zu kommen.
entries.resilience_summary: Der Fix ist widerstandsfaehig, weil nicht nur die fehlerhafte Zeilenreihenfolge korrigiert wurde, sondern der Bootstrap-Pfad jetzt auch aktiv von der PowerShell-Validierung ausgefuehrt wird. Dadurch wuerde derselbe Fehler bei spaeteren Aenderungen sofort wieder auffallen, statt erst auf einem neuen Rechner im echten Einsatz sichtbar zu werden.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Abhaengigkeiten: Die PowerShell-Validierung, das Bootstrap-Skript und spaetere Aufrufer muessen denselben Einstiegspunkt nutzen. Fehlszenario: Wenn erneut Code vor `param(...)` landet, muss die Validierung sofort fehlschlagen. Zustandsaenderung: Der Bootstrap installiert self-improve lokal, veraendert aber keine Fremd-Workspaces. Race Conditions sind gering, weil der Smoke sequentiell laeuft. Rueckwaertskompatibilitaet bleibt erhalten, da nur die interne Zeilenreihenfolge geaendert wurde. Plattform-Effekt: Der Fehler ist PowerShell-spezifisch und betrifft damit vor allem Windows bzw. pwsh-Aufrufe. Update-Resistenz entsteht durch den dauerhaften Bootstrap-Smoke. Graceful Degradation bleibt erhalten, weil der Fehler nun frueh in der Validierung statt spaet in einer realen Session sichtbar wird.
entries.verification: Nach der Korrektur lief `pwsh -NoProfile -File .\\codex-setup\\scripts\\validate-codex-setup.ps1` grün durch, der eingebaute Bootstrap-Smoke fuehrte `bootstrap-codex-setup.ps1 -SkipValidate` erfolgreich aus und die anschliessende Git-Bash-Validierung lief ebenfalls grün.
entries.portability_notes: Andere CLIs mit PowerShell-Komponenten sollten dieselbe Vermeidungsregel in ihre Skriptkonventionen und Bootstrap-Smokes uebernehmen. Der Fehler ist leicht wiederholbar, wenn neue `.ps1`-Bootstrap- oder Wrapper-Skripte aus allgemeinem Skriptstil heraus gebaut werden, ohne die besondere Position von `param(...)` zu beachten.

entries.summary: PowerShell bootstrap, install, and validator paths now run fail-closed on macOS.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix belongs to the macOS parity path for Codex setup. The local workspace had already been rebased onto the newer codex-setup state from origin/main, but the PowerShell validator still carried two macOS-specific blind spots that the older Bash path did not expose: relative .ps1 child-script invocations could fail as command-resolution errors without failing the validator, and the PowerShell self-improve installer still assumed a Windows-only USERPROFILE variable.
entries.symptom_before_fix: On macOS the PowerShell validation path printed errors such as The module codex-setup could not be loaded while still ending green, and the bootstrap path failed once it tried to install self-improve through PowerShell because Join-Path received a null USERPROFILE. That meant the setup looked validated even though the PowerShell parity path was not actually fail-closed.
entries.root_cause: The validator invoked some sibling .ps1 files via relative strings like codex-setup/scripts/... instead of absolute pwsh -File calls, so PowerShell on macOS treated those names as command lookups instead of stable file paths. At the same time install-self-improve.ps1 resolved its target directory only from USERPROFILE, which is empty in this macOS runtime although the real home directory is available through UserProfile APIs or HOME.
entries.what_was_fixed: validate-codex-setup.ps1 now runs PowerShell wrapper checks through a single absolute pwsh -File helper, and install-self-improve.ps1 resolves the user home directory through UserProfile/HOME fallback.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The macOS host showed that PowerShell parity could look green while wrapper calls or installer path resolution were actually broken.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom texts: PowerShell printed "The module 'codex-setup' could not be loaded" during validator self-tests, and install-self-improve.ps1 hit "Cannot bind argument to parameter 'Path' because it is null."
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the PowerShell validator did not fail when some wrapper scripts were not actually executed on macOS. Why 2: the script used path strings that were valid enough on Windows but ambiguous on macOS PowerShell command resolution, and it lacked a single checked helper for fail-closed wrapper execution. Why 3: the PowerShell side had not been hardened against cross-platform path semantics and home-directory resolution with the same rigor as the Bash side.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same error class across bootstrap-codex-setup.ps1, bootstrap-report.ps1, the OpenAI-docs and code-search PowerShell wrappers, and the self-improve installer because they belong to the same PowerShell parity surface. Also rechecked both full validators and the direct PowerShell installer path on macOS after the fix.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: invoke sibling .ps1 files through relative command strings and derive ~/.codex paths only from USERPROFILE.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: launch sibling PowerShell scripts through explicit absolute pwsh -File calls and resolve the user home directory through platform-aware fallbacks.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a Codex PowerShell validator or bootstrapper launches sibling scripts or targets the user home directory, then always use absolute pwsh -File execution and a platform-aware home fallback instead of Windows-only assumptions.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it centralizes PowerShell wrapper execution behind one checked helper, fail-closed because wrapper launch errors now stop validation immediately, portable because home-directory resolution no longer depends on Windows-only USERPROFILE, and update-resistant because future PowerShell wrapper calls can reuse the same absolute-path invocation pattern.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: PowerShell child-process launching, local wrapper scripts, and installer path resolution were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if a wrapper script is missing or exits non-zero, validation now stops loudly instead of drifting green. State changes: only the normal local self-improve deployment path is touched. Race conditions are negligible because the helper is synchronous. Backward compatibility remains because Windows PowerShell still accepts the absolute pwsh -File path. Platform effects improve because macOS now uses the same fail-closed path. Graceful degradation is preserved because failures abort validation before any false success is reported.
entries.verification: Verified by rerunning bash codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.sh, pwsh -NoProfile -File codex-setup/scripts/install-self-improve.ps1, and pwsh -NoProfile -File codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.ps1 on macOS after the changes. All three now complete successfully, and the earlier module-resolution / null-USERPROFILE failures no longer appear.
entries.portability_notes: Any CLI that validates sibling PowerShell scripts on macOS or Linux should launch them through explicit absolute pwsh -File calls and should resolve the user home directory through platform APIs plus HOME fallback instead of assuming USERPROFILE exists outside Windows.
entries.impact: The PowerShell parity path on macOS now fails only on real setup regressions instead of masking wrapper-launch and home-resolution bugs.

entries.summary: The codex-setup validators now give the OpenAI-Docs MCP smoke enough time on fresh macOS sessions.
entries.context_for_other_clis: This fix belongs to the Codex-side validation path after the directive-1 sync. The stronger directive text itself validated correctly, but the full PowerShell validator exposed that the OpenAI-Docs MCP smoke test could still time out on a healthy runtime because the fresh Codex exec needed slightly more wall-clock time to finish the docs query and emit AVAILABLE.
entries.symptom_before_fix: The PowerShell validator failed even though the fresh Codex exec started both MCP servers successfully and reached the openaiDeveloperDocs.search_openai_docs tool call. Both attempts then hit the 30000 ms budget and the validator stopped with an openaiDeveloperDocs MCP smoke test failure.
entries.root_cause: The repo validator treated the docs-MCP smoke as if the default 30000 ms timeout was always enough for a fresh Codex exec plus MCP startup plus first docs query. On this macOS host the integration was healthy, but the bounded validation budget was too tight for an occasionally slower first response.
entries.what_was_fixed: validate-codex-setup.sh and validate-codex-setup.ps1 now call the docs-MCP smoke wrapper with --timeout-ms 45000 during validation, while the standalone script default stays unchanged.
entries.why_it_was_fixed: The directive-1 sync was already correct, but the validator still produced a false negative because the docs-MCP smoke budget was too tight for a healthy fresh session.
entries.exact_error_text: Exact symptom text: Fresh Codex exec did not confirm openaiDeveloperDocs availability within the bounded retry window.
entries.root_cause_why_chain: Why 1: the validator failed although the MCP server was up. Why 2: the smoke test budget expired before the first docs query returned AVAILABLE. Why 3: the validation wrapper reused the generic timeout without extra slack for the fresh-process startup and first MCP roundtrip.
entries.related_error_sources_checked: Checked the same failure surface in both validate-codex-setup.sh and validate-codex-setup.ps1 because they both invoke the same docs-MCP smoke wrapper. Also checked that the standalone smoke script itself did not need a global default change, only a more tolerant validation-time invocation.
entries.wrong_pattern_example: Wrong pattern: use the same strict timeout for a fresh validator-launched MCP smoke and for every ordinary manual call regardless of startup jitter.
entries.right_pattern_example: Right pattern: keep the smoke bounded, but give validator-triggered fresh-session checks enough timeout headroom to distinguish real outages from startup latency.
entries.avoidance_rule: If a validator launches a fresh MCP-backed Codex session, then tune the validation-time timeout for realistic startup latency instead of treating every timeout as a product failure.
entries.resilience_summary: The fix is defensive because it hardens both Bash and PowerShell validators together, narrow because it changes only the validation call site instead of all user-facing defaults, and update-resistant because future docs-MCP runs still use the same wrapper while validation gets enough headroom for fresh-session startup jitter.
entries.fix_induced_failure_review: Dependencies: validator wrappers, fresh codex exec startup, MCP initialization, and the docs query roundtrip were reviewed together. Failure scenario: if the MCP is really broken, validation still fails, only later and for the right reason. State changes: none beyond the normal smoke test. Race conditions remain bounded because the timeout is still finite. Backward compatibility is preserved because the wrapper interface is unchanged. Platform effects improve because slower startup on macOS no longer causes a false negative. Graceful degradation remains intact because the validator still stops on a real docs-MCP failure.
entries.verification: Verified by rerunning bash codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.sh and pwsh -NoProfile -File codex-setup/scripts/validate-codex-setup.ps1 after raising the validation-time timeout to 45000 ms. Both validators completed successfully on this macOS host.
entries.portability_notes: Any CLI that validates a fresh MCP-backed docs query should keep the runtime smoke bounded but allow enough budget for first-process startup and first-tool latency. If a stricter timeout is desired for normal manual use, pass the longer timeout only from the validator wrapper instead of globally widening every invocation.
entries.impact: PowerShell and Bash validation are now less likely to report a false docs-MCP outage on healthy but slightly slower fresh sessions.
